name: Xerox PARC ubicomp — tabs / pads / boards
short_description: Xerox PARC's ubiquitous computing program and its prototype device family (ParcTab, ParcPad/MPad, LiveBoard) embodying the inch-scale tab, foot-scale pad, and yard-scale board taxonomy.
long_description: Beginning around 1988 under Mark Weiser, Xerox PARC pursued 'ubiquitous computing': the idea that computation should recede into the everyday environment as many networked devices at different scales rather than one personal computer. PARC built working prototypes for each of Weiser's three categories — inch-scale 'tabs' (the ParcTab, a palm-sized, infrared-networked, context-aware handheld), foot-scale 'pads' (the ParcPad and MPad pen tablets), and yard-scale 'boards' (the LiveBoard wall-sized interactive display). Articulated in Weiser's 1991 Scientific American article 'The Computer for the 21st Century', this work is the foundational genealogy for ambient, calm, and physically situated computing, and a direct ancestor of today's smart objects, tablets, and the Internet of Things.

images.caption: Xerox PARC ubiquitous computing prototypes: the ParcTab handheld, ParcPad tablet, and LiveBoard wall display.
images.alt_text: Period photographs of Xerox PARC's ubiquitous computing devices: a small handheld ParcTab, a pen-based ParcPad tablet, and a large wall-mounted LiveBoard interactive display.

sources.title: Ubiquitous computing
sources.access_notes: Overview of Weiser's tab/pad/board taxonomy (inch/foot/yard scales) and the PARCTab, PARCPad, and LiveBoard prototypes built at Xerox PARC, 1988–1994.

sources.title: An Overview of the PARCTab Ubiquitous Computing Experiment
sources.access_notes: Roy Want et al. describe the ParcTab palm-sized, infrared-networked, context-aware handheld as a testbed for ubiquitous computing at Xerox PARC.

sources.title: The Work and Vision of Ubiquitous Computing at Xerox PARC
sources.access_notes: Historical account of the tabs/pads/boards program and its prototype devices.

interactions.title: Point your tab at the room and dim the lights
interactions.description: Because the ParcTab knew which room it was in from the ceiling infrared transceivers, it doubled as a programmable remote control — you could adjust the lights and temperature for wherever you happened to be standing, with no fixed wall panel.
interactions.source_label: Want et al., IEEE Personal Communications (1995)

interactions.title: Ask the building where a colleague is
interactions.description: A 'where is' query on the tab returned the last room a person's tab had been seen in, turning the office into something you could search — find the meeting that already moved, or whether someone is at their desk before walking over.
interactions.source_label: Want et al., IEEE Personal Communications (1995)

interactions.title: Walk to another room and your work follows
interactions.description: Application state migrated with you: a document or session you started in one office could be picked back up on the nearest display when you moved, the system using your tab's location to hand the work off rather than tying it to one machine.
interactions.source_label: Want et al., IEEE Personal Communications (1995)

interactions.title: Draw on a wall in two cities at once
interactions.description: Two people at LiveBoards in different buildings shared one pen-driven surface over phone lines — sketch a diagram on the wall here and watch it appear, editable, on the wall there, the yard-scale 'board' acting as a networked whiteboard for a distributed meeting.

interactions.title: Vote and fill in forms from your palm during a meeting
interactions.description: In the live PARC deployment people used tab-based dynamic forms and polling during group sessions — answering a question on the handheld and having the tallied result appear on the shared board, an early taste of the audience-response patterns we now take for granted.
interactions.source_label: Want et al., IEEE Personal Communications (1995)

interactions.title: Glance, don't open: the tab as a peripheral display
interactions.description: Staff in the experiment leaned on the tab for ambient glances — weather, clock, a buzz when a message arrived — checking a fact at arm's length rather than sitting down at a workstation, the everyday version of Weiser's 'calm' computing the program was built to test.
interactions.source_label: Want et al., IEEE Personal Communications (1995)

interactions.title: The room greets the work, not the login
interactions.description: Extending the tab's location model: walk into a conference room and the nearest board could quietly pull up the agenda for the people now present and the lights they prefer — no badge tap or sign-in, the space reacting to who is in it. Grounded in the system's room-level location sensing and environment control, in the spirit of Weiser's Sal scenario.
